---
title: "Video game engines as the new virtual Skinner box"
date: 2026-01-08
author:
  - name: Michael E. Young
    affiliation: Office of the Provost, Baylor Univerisity
  - name: Patrick M. Hancock
    affiliation: Department of Psychological Sciences, Kansas State University
  - name: Lucas Watson
    orcid: 0009-0002-0637-4612
    affiliation: Department of Psychological Sciences, Kansas State University
  - name: Brian Howatt
    orcid: 0000-0001-9195-4712
    affiliation: Department of Psychological Sciences, Kansas State University
  - name: Robert Southern
    orcid: 0009-0000-9903-5729
    affiliation: Department of Psychological Sciences, Kansas State University
  - name: Karissa Payne
    affiliation: Department of Psychological Sciences, Kansas State University 
categories: 
  - Video games
  - Behavior
pub-info:
  reference: >-
    Michael E. Young, Patrick M. Hancock, <strong>Lucas Watson</strong>, Brian Howatt, Robert Southern, and Karissa Payne (January 2026). Video game engines as the new virtual Skinner box, <em>Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 125</em>(1), e70081. doi: <a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/jeab.70081" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer"><code>10.1002/jeab.70081.70081</code></a>.
  links:
  - name: Preprint (PDF)
    url: young_et_al_2026_skinner_box.pdf
    local: true
    icon: fa-solid fa-file-pdf
  - name: Final version
    url: https://doi.org/10.1002/jeab.70081
    icon: fa-solid fa-scroll
  - name: Add to Zotero
    url: https://www.zotero.org/save?type=doi&q=10.1002/jeab.70081
    icon: ai ai-zotero
doi: 10.1002/jeab.70081
---

## Citation

<p class="buttons" style="text-align:center;">
<a class="btn btn-danger" target="_blank" href="https://www.zotero.org/save?type=doi&q=10.1002/jeab.70081">{{< ai zotero >}}&emsp;Add to Zotero {{< fa square-arrow-up-right >}}</a>
</p>

```bibtex
@article{https://doi.org/10.1002/jeab.70081,
author = {Young, Michael E. and Hancock, Patrick M. and Watson, Lucas and Howatt, Brian and Southern, Robert and Payne, Karissa},
title = {Video game engines as the new “virtual” Skinner box},
journal = {Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior},
volume = {125},
number = {1},
pages = {e70081},
keywords = {behavioral variability, generalizability, Skinner box, video game},
doi = {https://doi.org/10.1002/jeab.70081},
url = {https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/jeab.70081},
eprint = {https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdf/10.1002/jeab.70081},
abstract = {Abstract The Skinner box has provided a standardized method of conducting experiments on operant behavior throughout the history of behavior analysis. As technology has advanced, these operant chambers have become increasingly complex to allow for the study of new stimuli, behavior, and outcomes. The present article takes this variability one step further by advocating for the use of video game engines in the study of operant behavior in humans. Game engines provide high levels of flexibility, control, and realism as well as continuous behavioral tracking, dynamic stimulus presentation, and complex reinforcement schedules that greatly expand the range of research questions that can be addressed. Importantly, the potential for increasing stimulus, response, and outcome variability provides the basis for assessing and maximizing the generalizability of operant and related principles. This article illustrates the use of video game engines to study causal inference, delay discounting, loot boxes, foraging, and multiplayer dynamics. Adopting game engines in behavioral research not only expands the scope of behavior analysis but also increases its relevance to real-world behavior, offering a promising path forward for innovation.},
year = {2026}
}
```
